landing.badge: The first science-based habit tracker app
landing.subtitle: The first habit tracking app based on real research. IF-THEN triggers, WOOP method, neuroplasticity. No "manifestation" nonsense.
landing.problemTitle: Why do most habit trackers fail?
landing.problemSubtitle: Because they focus on gamification instead of the actual habit mechanisms of your brain.
landing.featuresSubtitle: Every feature is backed by real research. No "vibes", no "manifestation". Science.
landing.featureIfThenDesc: Implementation intentions double your success rate (Gollwitzer 1999, d=0.65)
landing.featureWoopDesc: Wish, Outcome, Obstacle, Plan. 2x more activity (Oettingen)
landing.featureNeuroDesc: Track your brain's actual rewiring (Lally et al. 2010)
landing.featureExtinctionDesc: Detects dips after success. 24-36% of people experience this. THIS IS NORMAL!
landing.featureIdentityDesc: Change WHO you are, not just WHAT you do (James Clear - Atomic Habits)
landing.freeSubtitle: All features free. No limits. No hidden fees.
landing.ctaSubtitle: No credit cards. No BS. Just science that works.
landing.footer: © 2025 Habit Tracker. Built on science, not pseudoscience.

emptyState.title: You don't have any habits yet!
emptyState.subtitle: Habits are small actions you do regularly that will change your life over time. Let's create your first one!
emptyState.whatIsHabitDesc: A habit is an action you repeat so often it becomes automatic. Examples:
emptyState.example1: Drink a glass of water every morning
emptyState.step1Desc: Start with 1 habit, not 10! Once it becomes automatic (66 days), add another.
emptyState.step2Desc: Instead of "I'll exercise for an hour" try "I'll do 10 push-ups". Small steps = bigger success!
emptyState.step3Desc: "When [situation], then [action] in [place]" - this increases success rate by 65%!
emptyState.proTipText: The app automatically tracks Habit Strength (not just streaks), the 66-day neuroplasticity phase of your brain, and warns you about Extinction Bursts (a normal dip around week 3). All based on scientific research!

logEntry.notePlaceholder: How did it go today? Any notes?

week.subtitle: Your progress over the last 7 days

week.detailedDesc: Detailed calendar with entry editing and notes
week.noDataDesc: Start tracking habits and you'll see your weekly overview here
week.weekOverviewHelp: Visualization of your week. Green squares = completed, red borders = missed. Navigate between weeks!

identity.title: Identity Designer
identity.subtitle: Who do you want to become? Every habit is a vote for your new identity.
identity.whyDesc: Instead of goals like "I want to lose weight", focus on identity: "I am a healthy person". Research shows that identity change is more effective than focusing on outcomes. Every habit is a vote for your new identity.
identity.whySource: Source: James Clear - Atomic Habits, Research: Oyserman & Destin (2010)

createDialog.title: Create a new identity
createDialog.description: Define who you want to become. Every habit is a vote for this identity.
createDialog.identityHint: Instead of "I want to run" → "I am a runner"
createDialog.descriptionPlaceholder: Why is this identity important to you?
createDialog.scienceNote: Identity-based habits are more effective than outcome-based. Instead of "I want to lose weight" → "I am a healthy person". Every habit is a vote for your new identity.

card.noHabitsYet: No habits yet. Link habits to this identity!
card.deleteTitle: Are you sure you want to delete this identity?
card.deleteDesc: This action is irreversible. Identity "{name}" will be permanently deleted. Linked habits will be preserved but will lose their connection to this identity.

emptyState.title: You don't have any identity yet!
emptyState.subtitle: Identity-based habits are the most effective way to change behavior. Instead of "I want to run" -> "I am a runner"!
emptyState.whatIsIdentityDesc: Identity is WHO YOU ARE, not just what you do. When you change from within, behavior follows automatically.
emptyState.whyBetterTitle: Why does it work better than goals?
emptyState.intrinsicMotivationDesc: "I am a runner" -> you run because that's WHO YOU ARE, not because you "have to". That's much stronger!
emptyState.consistentDecisionsDesc: Every decision is a "vote" for your identity. "A healthy person would have a salad, not a burger."
emptyState.longTermChangeDesc: Goals are temporary ("I lost weight, now what?"). Identity is a permanent change!
emptyState.exampleRunnerDesc: linked habits: Run 3x a week, stretching, healthy diet
emptyState.exampleReaderDesc: linked habits: Read every evening, visit library, discuss books
emptyState.exampleCalmDesc: linked habits: Morning meditation, deep breathing, yoga
emptyState.researchClearQuote: "The highest form of intrinsic motivation is when a habit becomes part of your identity."
emptyState.researchOysermanDesc: Research showed that identity change leads to more lasting behavior changes than focusing on goals.

woop.title: WOOP Method
woop.subtitle: Wish · Outcome · Obstacle · Plan - Gabriele Oettingen

wizard.title: WOOP Method
wizard.description: Wish · Outcome · Obstacle · Plan - Gabriele Oettingen (2x activity increase)

stepWish.title: What do I want to achieve?
stepWish.description: Define your goal. Be specific and positive. Example: "I want to exercise every day for 30 minutes"

stepOutcome.title: What will be the best outcome?
stepOutcome.description: Visualize the positive outcome. How will you feel? What will you gain? Example: "I will have more energy, better fitness and confidence"
stepOutcome.label: Outcome *
stepOutcome.placeholder: When I achieve my goal, I will...
stepOutcome.tipDesc: Focus on emotions and specific changes. "I will feel..." is stronger than "I want to have..."

stepObstacle.title: What stands in my way?
stepObstacle.description: Identify the MAIN internal obstacle (not external circumstances). Example: "In the evening I'm tired and have no motivation"
stepObstacle.label: Obstacle *
stepObstacle.placeholder: What stops me most often is...
stepObstacle.importantDesc: The obstacle should be INTERNAL (feelings, thoughts), not external ("I don't have time" -> "I feel overwhelmed"). You can only control that.

stepPlan.title: When the obstacle comes, what will I do?
stepPlan.description: Create an IF-THEN plan. Example: "When I'm tired in the evening, I'll do just 10 minutes of light exercise instead of full 30"
stepPlan.label: Plan (IF-THEN) *
stepPlan.tipItem3: Be realistic - the plan must be achievable

card.title: WOOP Plan
card.deleteDesc: This action is irreversible. The WOOP plan will be permanently removed.
card.researchNote: Research: WOOP method by Gabriele Oettingen increases success rate 2x compared to positive visualization alone

emptyState.title: No WOOP plans yet!
emptyState.subtitle: The WOOP method doubles your chance of success by preparing you for obstacles in advance.
emptyState.whatIsWoopDesc: WOOP is a scientifically proven method by psychologist Gabriele Oettingen. It helps you achieve goals through 4 steps:
emptyState.wishQuestion: What do you want to achieve?
emptyState.planQuestion: How will you overcome the obstacle?
emptyState.whyWorksTitle: Why does WOOP work so well?
emptyState.realisticPlanningDesc: Instead of just positive thinking (which often fails), WOOP prepares you for real obstacles.
emptyState.mentalContrastingDesc: The combination of positive outcome + real obstacle creates stronger motivation in the brain.
emptyState.ifThenPlanningDesc: "When I encounter [obstacle], then [I'll do plan]" - automatic response to problems!
emptyState.researchDesc: Dr. Gabriele Oettingen (NYU): Research showed that the WOOP method doubled goal achievement success (2x more activity) compared to positive thinking alone.
emptyState.researchStudies: 93+ peer-reviewed studies confirm the effectiveness of mental contrasting + implementation intentions.
emptyState.exampleWish: I want to run 3x a week
emptyState.exampleOutcome: I will feel more energetic and healthier
emptyState.exampleObstacle: After work I'm tired and don't feel like it
emptyState.examplePlan: When I'm tired after work, I'll put on my running clothes as soon as I get home (before sitting down)

page.infoDesc: WOOP is a scientifically-backed method for achieving goals developed by psychologist Gabriele Oettingen. It combines positive thinking with realistic obstacle planning.
page.createForHabit: Create a WOOP plan for your habit
page.clickToCreate: Click to create a WOOP plan
page.createHabitFirstDesc: WOOP plans are created for specific habits. Start by creating your first habit.

cbt.title: Thought records
cbt.subtitle: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy — ABC model — Dr. Aaron Beck
cbt.howItWorks: How does a thought record work?
cbt.howItWorksDesc: When a negative thought comes to mind, write it down using the ABC model. Then look for evidence for and against, and create a more realistic perspective.
cbt.loadErrorDesc: Failed to load thought records. Please try again.

wizard.title: New thought record
wizard.description: Break down the situation using the ABC model of cognitive behavioral therapy.
wizard.adversityDesc: Describe the situation that triggered negative thoughts. Be specific — who, what, when, where.
wizard.adversityPlaceholder: E.g.: A colleague criticized me during a meeting in front of the whole team for a mistake in the report...
wizard.adversityTip: Write only facts — what objectively happened, not how you felt (that comes in the next step).
wizard.beliefDesc: What automatic thought came to mind? And how did you feel afterwards?
wizard.beliefPlaceholder: E.g.: "I'm incompetent, everyone thinks so, I'll never be able to do this..."
wizard.consequencePlaceholder: E.g.: Anger, anxiety, shame. I wanted to leave the office and be alone...
wizard.reframingDesc: Try to find evidence for and against your automatic thought, and then come up with a more realistic perspective.
wizard.evidencePlaceholder:
For: I made a mistake in the report, that's a fact.
Against: My boss praised me last week. I have good results for the past month.
wizard.alternativePlaceholder: E.g.: A mistake in the report doesn't mean I'm incompetent. Everyone makes mistakes. My colleague might have been having a bad day...
wizard.threePDesc: Martin Seligman found that pessimists see problems as permanent, pervasive, and personal. Optimists see the opposite.
wizard.permanencePlaceholder:
Pessimistic: "It will always be like this"
Optimistic: "Today was bad, but tomorrow will be better"
wizard.pervasivenessDesc: Does it affect everything, or just this situation?
wizard.pervasivenessPlaceholder:
Pessimistic: "I'm not good at anything"
Optimistic: "I made a mistake on this one task"
wizard.personalizationDesc: Is it entirely your fault, or are there external factors?
wizard.personalizationPlaceholder:
Pessimistic: "It's entirely my fault"
Optimistic: "There were external factors too — tight deadline, missing data"

card.title: Thought record
card.researchFooter: CBT improves stress management and habit building — Dr. Aaron Beck
card.deleteDesc: This thought record will be permanently deleted. This action cannot be undone.

emptyState.title: No thought records yet!
emptyState.subtitle: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) will help you recognize and change negative thought patterns.
emptyState.whatIsABCDesc: The ABC model is the foundation of cognitive behavioral therapy by Dr. Aaron Beck. It helps you understand why you feel what you feel:
emptyState.beliefQuestion: What do you think about it?
emptyState.consequenceQuestion: What do you feel and how do you react?
emptyState.step1Desc: You'll learn to catch negative thoughts before they influence your behavior.
emptyState.step2Desc: You'll check whether your thoughts match reality — often you'll find they don't.
emptyState.step3Desc: You'll create a more realistic and healthier perspective on the situation.
emptyState.researchDesc: Dr. Aaron Beck (1960s): Founder of cognitive behavioral therapy. CBT is one of the most researched forms of psychotherapy with 2000+ clinical studies confirming its effectiveness in treating depression, anxiety, and habit change.

addDialog.title: Add milestone
addDialog.description: Define a milestone you want to achieve with this identity.
addDialog.hint: Specific, measurable goal to celebrate progress
addDialog.tipDesc: Milestones help you celebrate progress and maintain motivation. Don't just focus on the end goal - small victories along the way are important!
addDialog.example4: Half a year as new identity

extinctionBurst.tooltipDesc: 24-36% of people experience a temporary dip after a good start. This is NORMAL! Your brain is resisting change. Keep going!
extinctionBurst.researchDesc: 24-36% of people experience an "extinction burst" - a temporary worsening before lasting improvement. Those who push through have 3x higher success rate in long-term habit maintenance!

welcome.step1Desc: An app based on scientific research that will actually help you change your habits
welcome.step2Desc: Every feature has a scientific basis - no motivational fluff
welcome.step3Desc: 3 simple steps to your first change
welcome.step4Desc: We'll create your first habit together
welcome.scientificallyProven: We use methods that are scientifically proven:
welcome.iiDesc: Instead of "I'll exercise" → "When I get home from work, I'll do 10 push-ups"
welcome.neuroDesc: Your brain needs an average of 66 days to create an automatic habit
welcome.createFirstHabitDesc: Start with ONE thing. Not 10 habits at once!
welcome.bePatientDesc: It takes 66 days for a habit to become automatic
welcome.readyDesc: Now we'll create your first habit with an IF-THEN plan
welcome.tipStartDesc: Not sure where to start? Try: "When I wake up, I'll drink a glass of water in the kitchen" or "When I go to bed, I'll read 5 pages of a book". Small steps = big changes!

tutorial.startWithOne: Let's start with ONE thing. Small steps = big changes
tutorial.learnTechnique: We'll teach you a technique that increases success by 65%
tutorial.beSpecific: Be specific - it will help you succeed
tutorial.researchOneFocus: Research shows that people who focus on ONE habit have 80% success rate. Those who start with 3+ habits? Only 35%.
tutorial.chooseOrCreate: Choose inspiration or create your own:
tutorial.fillNameAndAction: Please fill in at least the name and action
tutorial.goodExampleText: "When I get home from work, I'll do 10 push-ups in the living room" (specific, 65% success rate)
tutorial.whenExamples: "When I wake up", "When I sit down for dinner", "When I close my laptop"
tutorial.whatExamples: "I'll drink a glass of water", "I'll do 5 squats", "I'll read 1 page"
tutorial.whereExamples: "In the kitchen", "On the balcony", "At the desk"
tutorial.scientificDesc: Gollwitzer (1999): IF-THEN plans increase success by 65% (effect size d=0.65). Your brain better knows WHEN and HOW to act.
tutorial.habitNamePlaceholder: e.g. Drink water in the morning
tutorial.actionPlaceholder: I'll drink a glass of water...
tutorial.dashboardAppear: The habit will appear in your dashboard
tutorial.scienceMetrics: You'll see science-based metrics (66-day timeline, Habit Strength)
tutorial.extinctionSupport: You'll get support during Extinction Burst

metadata.title: Habit Tracker - Science-Based Approach to Habits
metadata.description: The first science-based habit tracking app. IF-THEN implementation intentions, WOOP method, neuroplasticity, and more research-backed features.
metadata.ogTitle: Habit Tracker - Science-Based Approach to Habits
metadata.ogDescription: The first habit tracking app built on science, not pseudoscience

review.weeklySubtitle: Overview of your progress and planning the next week
review.reflectionPrompt: Think about the past week and write your thoughts:
review.reflectionQ2: What obstacles did you have to overcome?
review.reflectionQ3: What would you change next week?
review.reflectionQ4: How do you feel about your progress?
review.reflectionResearch: Research shows that reflective writing increases self-confidence and long-term motivation.

recovery.title: Recovery Flow: {habitName}
recovery.subtitle: Guided recovery for extinction burst - based on research
recovery.brainTitle: What is happening in your brain
recovery.whyPercentage: Why does 24-36% of people do this?
recovery.brainExplanation: Your brain is going through an "extinction burst" phase - a behavioral phenomenon known from research. It works like this:
recovery.brainStep1: You were building new neural connections (new habit)
recovery.brainStep2: After ~3-4 weeks, the brain "asks": "Do we really need this?"
recovery.brainStep3: It tests you - increases resistance, makes it harder than before
recovery.brainStep4: If you persist, the brain accepts: "OK, this is the new normal"
recovery.goodSignalTitle: This is actually a GOOD SIGN!
recovery.goodSignalDesc: Research shows that extinction burst often happens RIGHT BEFORE the habit becomes automatic. You're close! This is the final test.
recovery.researchNote: Research: Behavioral psychology studies show extinction bursts occur in 24-36% of behavior change attempts, typically around week 4-8. Those who persist through this phase have 70%+ success rate for long-term habit adoption.
recovery.nextStepObstacles: I understand - Next step: Planning obstacles
recovery.woopDesc: The WOOP method helps anticipate obstacles and plan IF-THEN responses. This doubles the chance of success according to research.
recovery.woopPlaceholder: WOOP form will be implemented here
recovery.thoughtsDesc: Identify and refute automatic negative thoughts using the ABC model from CBT.
recovery.thoughtsPlaceholder: Thought Challenge form will be implemented here
recovery.completedSuccess: Recovery flow completed! You're back on the right track.

selfCompassion.basedOnResearch: Based on research by Kristin Neff - Step {step} of {total}
selfCompassion.commonHumanityDesc: 24-36% of people experience extinction burst. This is a normal part of the change process. We all struggle sometimes.
selfCompassion.commonHumanityPlaceholder: How does it feel knowing that others experience this too?
selfCompassion.selfKindnessQuestion: How would you talk to a friend?
selfCompassion.selfKindnessDesc: If you saw a good friend in this situation, what would you say? Would you be critical or compassionate?
selfCompassion.selfKindnessPlaceholder: Write what you would say to a friend (and then say it to yourself)...
selfCompassion.mindfulnessQuestion: What do you feel, without judgment
selfCompassion.mindfulnessDesc: Notice your feelings without judging them as good or bad. Just observe them.
selfCompassion.mindfulnessPlaceholder: What emotions do you feel right now? (frustration, disappointment, anxiety...)
selfCompassion.researchNote: Self-compassion increases resilience and reduces the risk of giving up after failure. People who practice self-compassion are more likely to continue with habits even after obstacles.

neuroplasticity.title: 66-Day Neuroplasticity
neuroplasticity.tooltipDesc: Your brain needs an average of 66 days (range 18-254) to create an automatic habit. You're not lazy, you just need time!
neuroplasticity.brainTitle: What is happening in your brain
neuroplasticity.phase4Desc: Habit is fully automatic and integrated
neuroplasticity.researchDesc: Lally et al. (2010) - European Journal of Social Psychology. A study of 96 participants showed that it takes an average of 66 days (range 18-254 days) for a new habit to become automatic. Neuroplastic adaptation continues even after reaching automaticity.

difficulty.bjFoggFormula: Behavior = Motivation x Ability x Prompt
difficulty.bjFoggDesc: When a habit is hard (low Ability), increasing motivation isn't enough. You need to simplify the habit!
difficulty.researchNote: Research: BJ Fogg (Stanford University) - Tiny Habits method. When a habit is too hard, the only solution is to make it smaller, not increase motivation.

analytics.title: Statistics & Analytics
analytics.subtitle: Detailed overview of your progress and performance
analytics.completionLast7: Completion rate for the last 7 days
analytics.above80: Your success rate is above 80%. Keep up this trend!
analytics.decentSuccess: You have a decent success rate. Try to focus on consistency.
analytics.everyStartHard: Every beginning is hard. Focus on small steps every day.
analytics.daysInRow: {count} day(s) in a row. Don't let the flame die!
